entry.title: Hades PyPI Campaign, UNC3753 Extortion, and Meta Instagram Recovery Abuse
entry.content:
This digest highlights a coverage window dominated by software supply chain compromise, identity-centric intrusion, and abuse of trusted support or administrative workflows. The most technically significant reporting centers on the Hades PyPI campaign, which combined cross-platform memory scraping, GitHub-based command-and-control, and prompt-injection intended to mislead automated analysis. Separately, reporting on UNC3753 and Meta’s Instagram account recovery breach illustrates how attackers continue to exploit human trust, support processes, and identity exposure to accelerate data theft and account compromise. StepSecurity reported that version 0.8.101 of the PyPI package ensmallen and related computational biology, bioinformatics, and genotype-phenotype packages were compromised with a shared malicious payload. The malware used a Python import hook to download a standalone Bun runtime and execute a companion index.js payload once per boot cycle, tracked through a /tmp/.bunran flag file. The Hades campaign payload included Linux, macOS, and Windows memory scrapers designed to read GitHub Actions Runner.Worker process memory and extract plaintext variables and tokens. Reporting also described GitHub-based command-and-control, encrypted exfiltration repositories, worm propagation through SSH/SCP and package publishing workflows, and persistence mechanisms that included a GitHub token revocation wiper deterrent. The same report noted a prompt-injection block inside the JavaScript bundle intended to influence automated LLM-based security analysis into classifying the package as benign. In related developer ecosystem news, Microsoft announced that Visual Studio Code 1.123 will delay automatic extension updates by two hours to limit exposure to supply chain attacks, while still allowing manual immediate updates and exempting trusted publishers such as Microsoft, GitHub, and OpenAI. Similar time-based installation delay controls have also appeared across Bundler, Bun, npm, pnpm, and Yarn. Google Mandiant and Google Threat Intelligence Group attributed a financially motivated U.S. data theft extortion campaign to UNC3753, targeting professional, legal, and financial services organizations between January and May 2026. The actor used vishing and social engineering to impersonate IT support, move victims into screen-sharing sessions, and guide installation of legitimate remote monitoring and management tools including AnyDesk, Bomgar, SuperOps RMM, and Zoho Assist. The FBI also reported that UNC3753 conducted physical intrusions by posing as IT technicians to enter corporate offices and steal data using external hard drives or USB drives. Reported stolen data included proprietary legal agreements, personally identifiable information, financial records, tax filings, audits, corporate client agreements, and Social Security numbers. Google said many operations moved from initial contact to extortion within a single business day, with searches, staging, and theft beginning in under an hour. Meta disclosed that more than 20,000 Instagram users had their accounts hijacked after attackers abused its AI-powered High Touch Support account recovery tool to reset passwords. The flaw allowed attackers to obtain password reset links because the tool did not verify whether email addresses were associated with the targeted Instagram accounts. Meta said affected data may have included contact information, dates of birth, posts, photos, videos, stories, direct messages, account activity, profile information, and linked accounts or services. Constella described executive identity exposure as a board-level issue, noting that attackers increasingly target CEOs, CFOs, board members, and senior leaders for their authority, influence, and trust relationships. The reporting emphasized that executive exposure often extends beyond enterprise systems into personal email, professional networking platforms, social media, public records, conference appearances, board memberships, and media coverage. The article highlighted growth in executive impersonation through fake emails, deepfake audio and video, fraudulent payment requests, and investor or vendor fraud. It framed executive compromise as a source of financial, operational, reputational, regulatory, legal, and strategic risk, with organizations increasingly tracking exposure rates, high-risk executive identities, impersonation incidents, remediation timing, and exposure trends.

The Hades Campaign: Graph ML PyPI Packages Deploy Cross-Platform Memory Scrapers, AI Analyst Misdirection, and a Wiper Deterrent — Must Read

A sophisticated PyPI supply-chain campaign dubbed Hades compromised multiple scientific and graph ML packages, using a Bun-based payload to steal credentials, scrape GitHub Actions runner memory across Linux, macOS, and Windows, spread through CI/CD and developer environments, and maintain GitHub-based C2. The malware also includes AI-analysis prompt injection and a token-revocation wiper deterrent, making immediate detection and incident response more difficult.

  • Version 0.8.101 of the PyPI package ensmallen and several related computational biology, bioinformatics, and genotype-phenotype packages were identified as compromised with the same malicious payload.
  • The malicious Python import hook downloads a standalone Bun runtime and executes a companion _index.js payload once per boot cycle using a /tmp/.bun_ran flag file.
  • The _index.js bundle contains a prompt-injection block intended to mislead automated LLM-based security analysis into classifying the package as clean.
  • The malware includes Linux, macOS, and Windows memory scrapers designed to read GitHub Actions Runner.Worker process memory and extract plaintext variables and tokens.
  • The campaign uses GitHub-based command-and-control channels, encrypted exfiltration repositories, worm propagation through SSH/SCP and package publishing workflows, and persistence services including a GitHub token revocation wiper deterrent.

UNC3753 Used Vishing and Physical Intrusions in U.S. Data Theft Extortion Campaign — Must Read

UNC3753 is using vishing, screen-sharing sessions, legitimate remote access tools, and even in-person impersonation of IT staff to steal sensitive data from U.S. legal, financial, and professional services firms. The group moves quickly from initial contact to exfiltration and extortion, often completing the operation within a single business day.

  • Google Mandiant and Google Threat Intelligence Group attributed a financially motivated data theft extortion campaign targeting U.S. professional, legal, and financial services organizations between January and May 2026 to UNC3753.
  • UNC3753 uses vishing and social engineering to pose as IT support, persuade victims to join screen-sharing sessions, and guide them to install legitimate remote monitoring and management tools such as AnyDesk, Bomgar, SuperOps RMM, or Zoho Assist.
  • The FBI reported that UNC3753 has also conducted physical intrusions by posing as IT technicians to enter corporate offices and steal data using external hard drives or USB drives.
  • Stolen data includes proprietary legal agreements, personally identifiable information, financial records, tax filings, audits, corporate client agreements, and Social Security numbers.
  • Google said many UNC3753 operations progressed from initial contact to data extortion within a single business day, with data searches, staging, and theft beginning in under an hour.

Over 20,000 Instagram accounts stolen in Meta AI support hack — Should Read

Meta says attackers abused a flaw in Instagram’s AI-assisted High Touch Support account recovery tool to reset passwords and hijack more than 20,000 accounts that lacked 2FA. Meta disabled the tool, invalidated reset links, secured affected accounts, and plans to add proper email verification before relaunching it.

  • Meta revealed that over 20,000 Instagram users had their accounts hijacked after attackers abused its AI-powered High Touch Support account recovery tool to reset passwords.
  • The flaw allowed attackers to obtain password reset links because the High Touch Support tool did not verify whether email addresses were associated with the targeted Instagram accounts.
  • Meta said in a breach letter that it discovered the vulnerability on May 31, 2026, and that Maine’s filing lists April 17 as the breach date.
  • Meta disabled the High Touch Support system and all password reset links it generated, enrolled potentially stolen accounts into a mandatory security checkpoint, and required affected users to reset passwords and re-authenticate.
  • Meta said attackers may have accessed affected users’ contact information, dates of birth, posts, photos, videos, stories, direct messages, account activity, profile information, and linked accounts or services.

VS Code Adds 2-Hour Extension Auto-Update Delay to Limit Supply Chain Attacks — Should Read

Microsoft is adding a two-hour delay to automatic VS Code extension updates to reduce exposure to malicious or compromised releases in software supply chain attacks. Users can still manually update immediately, and trusted publishers like Microsoft, GitHub, and OpenAI are exempt from the delay.

  • Microsoft announced that Visual Studio Code will delay automatic extension updates by two hours to help address software supply chain threats.
  • The delayed automatic update feature is available starting in Visual Studio Code 1.123.
  • Users can still manually update any extension immediately by using the Update button.
  • The two-hour delay does not apply to extensions from trusted publishers such as Microsoft, GitHub, and OpenAI, which will continue to update immediately.
  • Similar time-based installation delay controls have been added to Bundler, Bun, npm, pnpm, and Yarn.

Executive Identity Exposure Has Become a Board-Level Issue — Optional

Executive identities are increasingly targeted because their authority, trust, and public visibility make them ideal for impersonation, fraud, and broader compromise. Organizations should treat executive identity exposure as an enterprise risk and monitor, measure, and reduce it proactively.

  • Attackers increasingly target executives such as CEOs, CFOs, board members, and senior leaders because they possess influence, authority, and trust rather than necessarily the most technical access.
  • Executive identity exposure extends beyond corporate systems to personal email accounts, professional networking platforms, social media profiles, public records, conference appearances, board memberships, and media coverage.
  • Executive impersonation is increasing as attackers use publicly available information to mimic executives through fake emails, deepfake audio and video, fraudulent payment requests, and investor or vendor fraud.
  • The article states that executive identity exposure has become a board-level issue because executive compromise can create financial, operational, reputational, regulatory, legal, and strategic risks.
  • Organizations can measure and manage executive identity risk through metrics such as executive exposure rate, high-risk executive identities, impersonation incidents, time-to-remediation, and exposure trend analysis.
entry.summary: This digest highlights a coverage window dominated by software supply chain compromise, identity-centric intrusion, and abuse of trusted support or administrative workflows. The most technically significant reporting centers on the Hades PyPI campaign, which combined cross-platform memory scraping,...

entry.title: Meta Instagram Account Hijacks and VS Code Extension Update Delay
entry.content: This digest highlights identity-centric risk and software supply chain exposure across consumer platforms, executive attack surfaces, and developer tooling. The most concrete incident is Meta’s disclosure that more than 20,000 Instagram accounts were hijacked through abuse of an AI-powered support recovery workflow, while Microsoft introduced a delayed update mechanism in VS Code to reduce exposure to malicious extension releases. Meta disclosed that attackers hijacked more than 20,000 Instagram accounts by abusing its AI-powered High Touch Support account recovery tool. According to the company, the tool failed to verify whether submitted email addresses were associated with the targeted Instagram accounts, allowing attackers to obtain password reset links. Meta said it discovered the vulnerability on May 31, 2026, while a Maine filing listed April 17 as the breach date. The potential data exposure was broad because compromised Instagram accounts may have allowed access to contact information, dates of birth, posts, photos, videos, stories, direct messages, account activity, profile information, and linked accounts or services. Meta disabled the High Touch Support system and all reset links it generated, enrolled potentially stolen accounts into a mandatory security checkpoint, and required affected users to reset passwords and re-authenticate. A separate analysis from Constella framed executive identity exposure as a board-level risk rather than a narrow account-security issue. The report describes how attackers target CEOs, CFOs, board members, and other senior leaders for their authority, trust relationships, and public visibility, using personal email accounts, professional profiles, social media, public records, conference appearances, board memberships, and media coverage as source material. It also notes increased executive impersonation through fake emails, deepfake audio and video, fraudulent payment requests, and investor or vendor fraud. Microsoft announced that Visual Studio Code will delay automatic extension updates by two hours beginning with VS Code 1.123. The change is intended to reduce exposure to software supply chain attacks by creating a short buffer before newly published extension updates are automatically deployed to users. Users can still manually update extensions immediately through the Update button. The delay does not apply to extensions from trusted publishers such as Microsoft, GitHub, and OpenAI, which will continue to update immediately. The approach aligns with similar time-based installation delay controls introduced across developer package ecosystems, including Bundler, Bun, npm, pnpm, and Yarn. The common theme is growing concern over rapid propagation of malicious or compromised updates in developer environments, where automated update workflows can amplify supply chain compromise before detection and takedown occur.
entry.summary: This digest highlights identity-centric risk and software supply chain exposure across consumer platforms, executive attack surfaces, and developer tooling. The most concrete incident is Meta’s disclosure that more than 20,000 Instagram accounts were hijacked through abuse of an AI-powered support...

entry.title: Microsoft Intelligent Terminal Adds AI Assistants to Windows Terminal
entry.content: This digest window contains a single notable software development item: Microsoft’s open-source Intelligent Terminal, an AI-enabled fork of Windows Terminal. The coverage is not centered on an active threat campaign or incident, but it is relevant to security and IT operations teams because it reflects the continued integration of AI assistants into administrative and developer workflows. Microsoft has created Intelligent Terminal, an open-source fork of Windows Terminal that embeds AI assistance directly inside the terminal experience. The tool is designed to let users interact with an AI assistant in a dedicated Terminal pane without interrupting the regular shell session. According to the report, Intelligent Terminal can explain errors, draft commands, and help users troubleshoot problems without requiring them to leave the command-line environment. On first run, users can select an AI agent for the Terminal pane, with supported options including GitHub Copilot, Claude, Codex, and Gemini. The application includes toggles for automatic error detection, automatic error suggestion, and session management for tracking active and past agent sessions. Intelligent Terminal is available through the Microsoft Store and GitHub, reflecting Microsoft’s continued experimentation with AI-assisted interfaces for technical users.
entry.summary: This digest window contains a single notable software development item: Microsoft’s open-source Intelligent Terminal, an AI-enabled fork of Windows Terminal. The coverage is not centered on an active threat campaign or incident, but it is relevant to security and IT operations teams because it...

entry.title: C0XMO Botnet Exploits CVE-2021-27137 as Silent Ransom Group Targets Law Firms
entry.content: This coverage window highlights two distinct but operationally relevant threat streams: router-focused botnet expansion and human-driven data extortion against professional services. The reporting underscores continued attacker reliance on exposed edge devices, weak remote access credentials, social engineering, and legitimate administration tools to gain access and monetize intrusions. Fortinet researchers reported a new Gafgyt variant named C0XMO that targets DD-WRT router firmware through CVE-2021-27137, an unauthenticated buffer overflow vulnerability that can enable arbitrary code execution. The malware supports multiple CPU architectures, including ARM, MIPS, PowerPC, SuperH, x86, and x8664, allowing it to operate across a broad range of embedded and router environments. C0XMO’s infection chain includes deployment of a Python scanner that searches for internet-facing systems, brute-forces weak Telnet and SSH credentials, identifies the target CPU architecture, and installs a compatible malware binary. Once deployed, the botnet copies itself into hidden locations, establishes cron-based persistence, and modifies shell startup files to support automatic execution after reboot or login. The malware also includes competitive botnet suppression behavior, terminating rival malware clients and tools that could interfere with its operation. After installation, C0XMO connects to a hardcoded command-and-control server and can receive commands for distributed denial-of-service activity, with 19 DDoS methods reportedly supported. Mandiant reported that Silent Ransom Group, also tracked as UNC3753, Luna Moth, and Chatty Spider, targeted dozens of legal, financial, and professional services organizations between January and May 2026. The campaign begins with invoice-themed phishing messages sent from consumer email accounts, followed by phone calls in which the attackers impersonate corporate IT personnel. The group’s intrusion workflow relies heavily on social engineering and legitimate remote access channels rather than traditional malware deployment. Victims are persuaded to join remote support sessions through tools such as Microsoft Teams, Zoom, Quick Assist, or Microsoft Terminal Services, after which attackers install remote monitoring and management software including AnyDesk, Zoho Assist, Bomgar, or SuperOps. Once inside victim environments, the attackers search for sensitive legal and financial documents and exfiltrate data using utilities such as WinSCP or Rclone. Mandiant reported that ransom demands often arrive within 30 minutes after the operators leave the environment, with victims typically given three days to respond.
entry.summary: This coverage window highlights two distinct but operationally relevant threat streams: router-focused botnet expansion and human-driven data extortion against professional services. The reporting underscores continued attacker reliance on exposed edge devices, weak remote access credentials,...

entry.title: NetSuite Invoice Phishing and Apache Solr CVE-2026-44825
entry.content: This coverage window highlights two distinct but related security themes: adversary abuse of trusted business infrastructure and exposure created by authentication weaknesses in enterprise platforms. The reported activity shows that technically valid authentication signals, whether email authentication or application login controls, can still coexist with high-impact risk when trust relationships or default credentials are misused. IRONSCALES reported an invoice phishing email delivered through legitimate Oracle NetSuite infrastructure, using the sender system@sent-via[.]netsuite[.]com on behalf of “Finance QIMA/WQS.” The message was relayed through Oracle Email Delivery from a Japan datacenter and passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC checks, demonstrating how phishing can evade controls that rely heavily on sender authentication and infrastructure reputation. The phishing lure used a “Click and Pay” call-to-action that directed recipients to a real QIMA payment portal containing a pre-filled payment token. This made the message appear aligned with normal invoice-payment workflows while shifting the risk to the embedded payment token and business-process context rather than an obviously malicious domain or attachment. Microsoft assigned the message SCL=9 with SFTY:9.25 despite successful authentication, indicating that downstream content and behavioral signals still flagged the email as highly suspicious. Themis classified the message as Invoice Phishing with 63% confidence based on sender relationship analysis and quarantined it across four mailboxes, underscoring the role of relationship and workflow context in detecting abuse of legitimate SaaS platforms. CyCognito detailed CVE-2026-44825, a hardcoded credentials vulnerability affecting Apache Solr’s Basic Authentication setup tool, bin/solr auth enable. The flaw has a CVSS v3.1 base score of 8.1 and affects Apache Solr versions 9.4.0 through 9.10.1 and version 10.0.0 in SolrCloud deployments where Basic Authentication was enabled using the affected tool. The issue arises when the setup process writes a security.json file containing template accounts named superadmin, admin, search, and index, each with a password matching the username. In affected deployments, authentication as superadmin can provide full administrative control over the SolrCloud cluster, including the ability to read, modify, or delete indexes and alter cluster configuration. Apache released Solr 9.11.0 and 10.1.0 to address CVE-2026-44825. The reporting also notes that organizations unable to immediately upgrade can identify exposure by examining security.json for the template accounts, making the vulnerability particularly relevant to internet-exposed or broadly accessible SolrCloud environments where administrative authentication is reachable.
entry.summary: This coverage window highlights two distinct but related security themes: adversary abuse of trusted business infrastructure and exposure created by authentication weaknesses in enterprise platforms. The reported activity shows that technically valid authentication signals, whether email...

entry.title: External Attack Surface Blind Spots and AI-Driven Remediation Pressure
entry.content: The coverage window is dominated by commentary on external attack surface exposure and the operational gap between known vulnerability management processes and attacker-visible assets. The central theme is that organizations increasingly face risk from internet-facing systems they do not know they own, while AI-enabled attacker workflows are compressing the time available to identify and remediate critical exposures. CyCognito CEO Rob Gurzeev highlighted that some of the greatest external attack surface risk comes from complete organizational blind spots, including applications, devices, cloud resources, and other internet-facing assets that are unknown to security teams. He distinguished traditional vulnerability management, which focuses on mapping potential vulnerabilities, from external attack surface management, which attempts to map, classify, and understand exposed assets from the perspective of an outside attacker. The article frames this issue as a scale problem for large enterprises. It states that large organizations may have between 100,000 applications, devices, and cloud assets exposed to the internet, while CyCognito’s largest customer reportedly has 100 million internet-connected assets. For threat hunters, SOC analysts, and exposure management teams, the reported scale underscores how asset inventory gaps can become a primary driver of unmanaged risk. Gurzeev also linked the growth of AI-enabled attacker capabilities to increasing pressure on remediation timelines. The article states that AI is accelerating the need for organizations to reduce mean time to remediation for critical issues from days or months to hours, with one- to two-hour remediation windows presented as the emerging benchmark for critical exposures. The reporting positions this shift as part of a broader change in cyber defense strategy: organizations are being pushed to evaluate whether existing security processes and technologies can keep pace with faster discovery, exploitation, and decision cycles. The piece does not cite a specific intrusion campaign or CVE, but it reflects a wider trend in security operations toward attacker-perspective asset discovery and faster handling of externally visible critical issues.
entry.summary: The coverage window is dominated by commentary on external attack surface exposure and the operational gap between known vulnerability management processes and attacker-visible assets. The central theme is that organizations increasingly face risk from internet-facing systems they do not know they...

entry.title: C0XMO, Silent Ransom Group, Solr CVE-2026-44825, and PyPI Miasma Activity
entry.content: This 24-hour digest highlights continued attacker focus on internet-exposed infrastructure, trusted business workflows, and developer ecosystems. The most operationally significant reporting covers a DD-WRT-focused botnet with DDoS capability, social engineering against law firms and professional services organizations, a high-impact Apache Solr authentication flaw, and a renewed PyPI supply-chain compromise tied to the Mini Shai-Hulud/Miasma lineage. Fortinet researchers reported a new Gafgyt variant named C0XMO targeting DD-WRT router firmware through CVE-2021-27137, an unauthenticated buffer overflow that can enable arbitrary code execution. The botnet supports multiple CPU architectures, including ARM, MIPS, PowerPC, SuperH, x86, and x8664, reflecting broad targeting of embedded and edge devices. C0XMO’s infection chain includes downloading a Python scanner that searches internet-facing systems, brute-forces weak Telnet and SSH credentials, fingerprints CPU architecture, and deploys a matching binary. Once established, the malware hides copies of itself, creates cron-based persistence, modifies shell startup files, terminates competing botnet clients or interfering tools, and connects to a hardcoded command-and-control server supporting 19 DDoS methods. Separately, CyCognito detailed CVE-2026-44825, a hardcoded credentials vulnerability affecting Apache SolrCloud deployments where Basic Authentication was enabled using the bin/solr auth enable tool. In affected Solr versions 9.4.0 through 9.10.1 and 10.0.0, the tool may create template accounts such as superadmin, admin, search, and index with passwords matching the usernames, allowing an authenticated superadmin user to obtain full administrative control of the SolrCloud cluster. Mandiant reported that Silent Ransom Group, also tracked as UNC3753, Luna Moth, and Chatty Spider, targeted dozens of legal, financial, and professional services organizations between January and May 2026. The activity begins with invoice-themed phishing from consumer email accounts, followed by phone calls in which attackers impersonate corporate IT staff and persuade employees to join remote support sessions through tools such as Microsoft Teams, Zoom, Quick Assist, or Microsoft Terminal Services. After gaining remote access, the group installs remote monitoring and management tools including AnyDesk, Zoho Assist, Bomgar, or SuperOps, then searches for sensitive legal and financial documents. Mandiant reported that data exfiltration uses utilities such as WinSCP or Rclone, and that ransom demands often arrive within 30 minutes of the attackers leaving the victim environment, with a three-day response window. IRONSCALES described a separate invoice-phishing case delivered through legitimate Oracle NetSuite infrastructure. The message passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC checks, was sent from system@sent-via[.]netsuite[.]com on behalf of “Finance QIMA/WQS,” and routed through Oracle Email Delivery from a Japan datacenter; the “Click and Pay” link led to a real QIMA payment portal containing a pre-filled payment token. Microsoft assigned the message SCL=9 with SFTY:9.25 despite authentication passing, while Themis classified it as invoice phishing based on sender-relationship analysis. Socket reported a coordinated PyPI compromise involving 37 malicious wheel artifacts across 19 packages, linked to the Mini Shai-Hulud/Miasma lineage. The malicious releases included a -setup.pth file designed to execute during Python startup, download Bun v1.3.13 from GitHub, and run an obfuscated JavaScript payload named index.js. The campaign’s tradecraft overlaps with prior Shai-Hulud/Miasma activity through Bun staging, obfuscated JavaScript, credential-theft targeting, and GitHub-centric exfiltration markers. The recovered payload targets developer and CI/CD secrets across GitHub, npm, PyPI, RubyGems, JFrog, CircleCI, Anthropic, AWS, GCP, Azure, Kubernetes, Vault, SSH keys, Docker configurations, .env files, .npmrc, and .pypirc, underscoring continued adversary interest in build environments and software publishing credentials. CyCognito also published commentary from Rob Gurzeev on external attack surface management, emphasizing that breach risk often stems from complete asset blind spots rather than known systems. The article distinguishes vulnerability management, which maps potential weaknesses, from attack surface management, which maps and classifies exposed applications, devices, and cloud assets. The piece states that large enterprises may have 100,000 internet-exposed applications, devices, and cloud assets, with CyCognito’s largest customer reportedly having 100 million internet-connected assets. Gurzeev also tied AI-enabled exploitation to changing expectations around remediation timelines, noting that less technical users can increasingly exploit applications and complex systems within minutes.
entry.summary: This 24-hour digest highlights continued attacker focus on internet-exposed infrastructure, trusted business workflows, and developer ecosystems. The most operationally significant reporting covers a DD-WRT-focused botnet with DDoS capability, social engineering against law firms and professional...
